title: Reading and Use of English - Part 6
description: You are going to read four extracts from internet articles about technology in sports. For questions 37 – 40, choose from the experts A – D. The expert may be chosen more than once.

content.title: Disposable Style: The Fast Fashion Debate

texts.A: The fashion industry is currently responsible for 10% of global carbon emissions, yet the conversation often focuses on the consumer's 'right' to cheap clothes. The workers at the bottom of the supply chain—predominantly women in the Global South—bear the greatest burden of this so-called accessibility, toiling in unsafe conditions for wages that barely cover subsistence. [37]While some argue that fast fashion democratizes style, I believe it is built on a foundation of human rights abuses that we can no longer ignore.[/37] [39]Furthermore, the idea that 'sustainable' collections from major retailers are a solution is laughable; it is merely greenwashing designed to keep us consuming.[/39] The only real fix is a radical reduction in the volume of clothes produced, moving back toward a 'slow fashion' model where quality is valued over quantity. We must hold corporations accountable through binding legislation and enforceable supply chain audits, not voluntary pledges that serve primarily as marketing tools for brands unwilling to relinquish their profit margins.
texts.B: We must be careful not to demonize an industry that provides millions of jobs in developing nations. For many workers in Bangladesh, Cambodia, and Ethiopia, fast fashion employment represents a genuine ladder out of extreme poverty, providing stable income and formal sector experience that might otherwise be unavailable. For many, fast fashion is not a 'choice' but an economic necessity. [38]I disagree with those who claim that the industry cannot be reformed from within; many brands are making genuine strides in water conservation and recycled materials.[/38] [40]The real challenge is the lack of textile recycling infrastructure; if we could create a truly circular economy for fabric, the speed of production would be less of an ecological concern.[/40] Innovation, not abstinence, is the way forward. Investing in scalable recycling systems and bio-based textiles will create the next generation of green manufacturing jobs while enabling consumers of all income levels to enjoy affordable clothing without the environmental guilt that currently accompanies every purchase.
texts.C: The psychological impact of fast fashion is as damaging as its environmental one. We are being trained to see clothes as disposable, leading to a 'hedonic treadmill' where we constantly seek the next purchase. This relentless cycle of acquisition and disposal has eroded our capacity to form meaningful attachments to the objects we own, creating a generation incapable of appreciating durability or craftsmanship. [37]I share the view that the democratization of fashion has come at too high a human and environmental cost.[/37] [39]I also agree that most corporate sustainability initiatives are performative, serving to distract from the fundamentally unsustainable nature of a business model based on infinite growth on a finite planet.[/39] We need to rediscover the art of mending and the dignity of wearing things for a decade, not a week. Schools, community organizations, and cultural institutions all have a role to play in rebuilding the skills and attitudes that fast fashion has systematically dismantled.
texts.D: The backlash against fast fashion is often tinged with a certain classism. It's easy to advocate for 'slow fashion' when you can afford a £100 t-shirt. Condemning those who choose budget clothing is not a progressive stance; it is an exercise in middle-class moralizing that conveniently ignores the economic realities facing the majority of households. [38]I share the view that the industry is moving in the right direction, driven by consumer demand for transparency.[/38] [40]However, I am less convinced than some that 'circularity' is a magic bullet; the energy required to break down and re-weave fibers is often as high as producing new ones.[/40] The focus should be on fair wages and safe working conditions, ensuring that the people who make our clothes are the primary beneficiaries of the industry's success. Redirecting our moral outrage toward wage justice would achieve far more tangible good than shaming those who cannot afford the alternatives.

questions.question: Which expert shares A's view on the moral cost of the 'democratization' of fashion?
questions.explanation: A believes the democratization is built on 'human rights abuses.' C shares this, stating that democratization has come at 'too high a human and environmental cost.'

questions.question: Which expert expresses a similar opinion to D regarding the possibility of the fashion industry improving its practices?
questions.explanation: D believes the industry is 'moving in the right direction.' B also 'disagrees with those who claim the industry cannot be reformed' and points to 'genuine strides' being made.

questions.question: Which expert shares A's skepticism about the sustainability claims made by large fashion corporations?
questions.explanation: A calls these collections 'laughable' and 'greenwashing.' C also agrees that they are 'performative' and a distraction from the unsustainable business model.

questions.question: Which expert has a different view from B on the potential of a 'circular economy' to solve fashion's environmental issues?
questions.explanation: B thinks that with circular infrastructure, production speed would be 'less of a concern.' D is 'less convinced' that circularity is a 'magic bullet,' citing the high energy costs of recycling fibers.
